name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
الٓمٓصٓ (١)
```

```english
Alif, Lam, Mim, Suad. (1)
```

```arabic
كِتَـٰبٌ أُنزِلَ إِلَيْكَ فَلَا يَكُن فِى صَدْرِكَ حَرَجٌ مِّنْهُ لِتُنذِرَ بِهِۦ وَذِكْرَىٰ لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (٢)
```

```english
[This is] a Book that has been sent down to you—so let there be no disquiet in your heart on its account that you may warn thereby—and as an admonition for the faithful. (2)
```

```arabic
ٱتَّبِعُوا۟ مَآ أُنزِلَ إِلَيْكُم مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ وَلَا تَتَّبِعُوا۟ مِن دُونِهِۦٓ أَوْلِيَآءَ ۗ قَلِيلًا مَّا تَذَكَّرُونَ (٣)
```

```english
Follow what has been sent down to you from your Lord, and do not follow any masters besides Him. Little is the admonition that you take! (3)
```

```arabic
وَكَم مِّن قَرْيَةٍ أَهْلَكْنَـٰهَا فَجَآءَهَا بَأْسُنَا بَيَـٰتًا أَوْ هُمْ قَآئِلُونَ (٤)
```

```english
How many a town We have destroyed! Our punishment came to it at night, or while they were taking a midday nap. (4)
```

```arabic
فَمَا كَانَ دَعْوَىٰهُمْ إِذْ جَآءَهُم بَأْسُنَآ إِلَّآ أَن قَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّا كُنَّا ظَـٰلِمِينَ (٥)
```

```english
Then their cry, when Our punishment overtook them, was only that they said, ‘We have indeed been wrongdoers!’ (5)
```

```arabic
فَلَنَسْـَٔلَنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ أُرْسِلَ إِلَيْهِمْ وَلَنَسْـَٔلَنَّ ٱلْمُرْسَلِينَ (٦)
```

```english
We will surely question those to whom the apostles were sent, and We will surely question the apostles. (6)
```

```arabic
فَلَنَقُصَّنَّ عَلَيْهِم بِعِلْمٍ ۖ وَمَا كُنَّا غَآئِبِينَ (٧)
```

```english
Then We will surely recount to them with knowledge, for We had not been absent. (7)
```

```arabic
وَٱلْوَزْنُ يَوْمَئِذٍ ٱلْحَقُّ ۚ فَمَن ثَقُلَتْ مَوَٰزِينُهُۥ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْمُفْلِحُونَ (٨)
```

```english
The weighing [of deeds] on that Day is a truth. As for those whose deeds weigh heavy in the scales—it is they who are the felicitous. (8)
```

```arabic
وَمَنْ خَفَّتْ مَوَٰزِينُهُۥ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلَّذِينَ خَسِرُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَهُم بِمَا كَانُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا يَظْلِمُونَ (٩)
```

```english
As for those whose deeds weigh light in the scales—it is they who have ruined their souls, because they used to wrong Our signs. (9)
```

## (Commentary)

The surah encompasses a general purpose that combines what is contained in the surahs beginning with the disjointed letters (الحروف المقطعة) "*Alif-Lam-Mim*" (الم) and the surah beginning with the letter "*Sad*" (ص). Keep this in mind until we fully exhaust the necessary discussion regarding the disjointed letters of the Quran at the beginning of Surah *Ha-Mim-’Ayn-Sin-Qaf*, if Allah the Exalted wills.

The surah seems to take the divine covenant (العهد الإلهي) — *the primordial pledge taken from humanity to worship Allah alone and associate nothing with Him* — and examines what became of it throughout the course of human history across nations and generations. Most of them broke and forgot it; then, when reminding signs (آيات) or prophets came to them calling them back to it, they denied and acted unjustly toward them, and none took heed except a few.

This is because the divine covenant — which is the summary of what the divine religious call entails — when it descends upon humanity (and human natures differ in their readiness to accept or reject), it inevitably transforms according to the places of its descent and the conditions and circumstances surrounding human souls. In some souls — those pure souls remaining upon their primordial nature (الفطرة) — it produced guidance toward faith in Allah and His signs. In others — the majority, whose souls incline toward the earth and are immersed in worldly desires — it produced the opposite: disbelief and rebellion.

This, in turn, entailed specific divine favors for the believers, such as success (توفيق), victory, and conquest in this world, and salvation from the Fire and triumph with Paradise and its eternal delights in the Hereafter. Conversely, it entailed wrath and a curse descending upon the disbelievers, and a befalling punishment that destroys their collective, cuts off their progeny, extinguishes their fire, turns them into mere tales, and tears them into utter pieces; and the punishment of the Hereafter is more disgraceful, and they shall not be helped.

This is the way (سنة) of Allah that has passed among His servants, and thus shall it continue to run; Allah judges, and there is none to reverse His judgment, and He is upon a straight path (صراط مستقيم).

The details of this divine way (السنة), when described to a people to call them toward faith in Allah and His signs, serve as a warning (إنذار) to them. When described to a believing people who possess a general knowledge of their Lord and an awareness of His Lordship (المقام الربوبي), it serves as a reminder (تذكرة) to them of Allah’s signs and an instruction in the necessary knowledge (المعارف) — namely, the knowledge of Allah, His Beautiful Names (الأسماء الحسنى), His Sublime Attributes (الصفات العليا), and His way that runs in the Hereafter and the first life. This is what is alluded to by His saying, the Exalted, in the second verse of the surah: **لِتُنْذِرَ بِهِ وَذِكْرى لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ** "that you may warn thereby, and as an admonition to the believers," indicating that its purpose is warning and reminder.

Although the surah is Meccan (مكية) — except for certain verses over which there is disagreement — the address within it is naturally directed toward the polytheists and a small group who believed in the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله. As appears from the verses at its beginning and end, it is a warning to people in general through its arguments, exhortations, and lessons, including the story of Adam عليه‌السلام and Iblis, and the stories of Noah, Hud, Salih, Lot, Shu’ayb, and Moses عليه‌السلام. It is also a reminder for the believers, reminding them of the knowledge (المعارف) contained within the summary of their faith regarding the Origin (المبدأ) and the Return (المعاد), and the realities that constitute divine signs.

The surah contains a high degree of divine knowledge (المعارف الإلهية), including the description of Iblis and his tribe, the description of the Hour (الساعة), the Balance (الميزان), the Elevations (الأعراف), the world of pre-existence (عالم الذر), the Covenant (الميثاق), the description of those who remember Allah, the mention of the Throne (العرش), the Manifestation (التجلي), the Beautiful Names (الأسماء الحسنى), and the mention that the Quran has an ultimate interpretation (تأويل), among other things.

It also includes a summary mention of obligations and prohibitions, such as His saying: **قُلْ أَمَرَ رَبِّي بِالْقِسْطِ** "Say, ‘My Lord has enjoined justice’" (Verse 29); His saying: **إِنَّما حَرَّمَ رَبِّيَ الْفَواحِشَ ما ظَهَرَ مِنْها وَما بَطَنَ** "Say, ‘My Lord has only forbidden indecencies, the outward among them and the inward’" (Verse 33); and His saying: **قُلْ مَنْ حَرَّمَ زِينَةَ اللهِ الَّتِي أَخْرَجَ لِعِبادِهِ وَالطَّيِّباتِ مِنَ الرِّزْقِ** "Say, ‘Who has forbidden the adornment of Allah which He has brought forth for His servants, and the good things of [His] provision?’" (Verse 32). Thus, its revelation preceded the revelation of Surah al-An’am, which contains His saying: **قُلْ لا أَجِدُ فِي ما أُوحِيَ إِلَيَّ مُحَرَّماً عَلى طاعِمٍ يَطْعَمُهُ** "Say, ‘I do not find in what has been revealed to me anything forbidden to be eaten by one who wishes to eat it’" (Al-An’am: 145). The apparent meaning of that verse is that the ruling of permissibility for what was not excepted from the prohibitions had been revealed before that surah; thus, the reference there is to what is in this surah.

Furthermore, the rulings and laws (الشرائع) mentioned in this surah are more concise and summarized than those mentioned in Surah al-An’am in His saying: **قُلْ تَعالَوْا أَتْلُ ما حَرَّمَ رَبُّكُمْ عَلَيْكُمْ** "Say, ‘Come, I will recite what your Lord has forbidden you’" (the verses). This supports the fact that this surah was revealed before al-An’am, according to the established method of legislating rulings in Islam gradually, moving from the general (الإجمال) to the detailed (التفصيل).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **المص كِتابٌ أُنْزِلَ إِلَيْكَ فَلا يَكُنْ فِي صَدْرِكَ حَرَجٌ مِنْهُ لِتُنْذِرَ بِهِ وَذِكْرى لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ** "Alif, Lam, Mim, Sad. [This is] a Book sent down to you; so let there be no impediment in your breast because of it, that you may warn thereby, and as an admonition to the believers." The indefiniteness of "Book" (كتاب) and its description as being "sent down to you" without mentioning the agent of the sending are all for the purpose of indicating magnification (التعظيم). The description of the Book and its Sender is further specified by the consequence (التفريع) in His saying: **فَلا يَكُنْ فِي صَدْرِكَ حَرَجٌ مِنْهُ** "so let there be no impediment in your breast because of it." It is as if it were said: This is a blessed Book that narrates the signs of Allah, which your Lord has sent down to you; so let there be no constriction (حرج) in your breast because of it. For if it were a book other than this Book, and your Lord had cast it upon you, it would be expected that your breast would feel constricted and tight due to the hardships and trials involved in conveying it and calling people to the guidance it contains.

His saying: **لِتُنْذِرَ بِهِ** "that you may warn thereby" is the objective (غاية) of the sending down and is connected to it, as is His saying: **وَذِكْرى لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ** "and as an admonition to the believers." Restricting the "reminder" (الذكرى) to the believers is evidence that the "warning" (الإنذار) encompasses them and others. Thus, the meaning is: The Book was sent down to you so that you may warn the people with it, and it is a reminder specifically for the believers because they take heed through the signs and divine knowledge mentioned therein regarding the station of their Lord; thus, their faith increases and their eyes are comforted. As for the general public, this Book affects them through warning, by containing the mention of Allah’s wrath and His punishment for the wrongdoers in the Hereafter, and in this world through the punishment of extirpation (عذاب الاستئصال), as explained by the stories of past nations.

From this, it becomes clear that the view of some — that His saying **لِتُنْذِرَ بِهِ** is connected to "impediment" (الحرج), meaning: "let there be no impediment in your breast for warning with it" — is not sound. This is because its being followed by His saying **وَذِكْرى لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ**, with the meaning we have established, refutes that.

It also becomes clear what is problematic in the apparent view of some: that "the believers" refers to everyone who was actually a believer at the time of revelation and those whom Allah knew would eventually believe. For the "reminder" (الذكرى) mentioned in the verse is only realized in one who is actually a believer.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **اتَّبِعُوا ما أُنْزِلَ إِلَيْكُمْ مِنْ رَبِّكُمْ وَلا تَتَّبِعُوا مِنْ دُونِهِ أَوْلِياءَ قَلِيلاً ما تَذَكَّرُونَ** "Follow what has been sent down to you from your Lord, and do not follow any masters besides Him. Little is the admonition you take!" When He mentioned to His Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله that it is a Book sent down to him for the purpose of warning, He began the warning and shifted from addressing him صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله to addressing them; for warning, by its nature, is done by addressing those being warned (المنذرين). The purpose of addressing the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله had already been achieved.

He addressed them with the command to follow what was sent down to them from their Lord, which is the Quran that commands them toward the right of belief and the right of action — meaning faith in Allah and His signs, and the righteous deeds (العمل الصالح) that Allah, the Glorified, commands in His Book and forbids their opposites. The sentence — namely His saying: **اتَّبِعُوا ما أُنْزِلَ إِلَيْكُمْ مِنْ رَبِّكُمْ** — is structured as a metonymy (كناية), serving as a metonym for entering under the guardianship (ولاية) of Allah, the Glorified. The evidence for this is His saying: **وَلا تَتَّبِعُوا مِنْ دُونِهِ أَوْلِياءَ** "and do not follow any masters besides Him," where He did not say, in the position of contrast: "and do not follow other than what was sent down to you."

**The meaning is:** Do not follow other than Him, the Exalted — and they are many — such that they become guardians (أولياء) for you besides Allah. Little do you take heed; for if you were to take heed, you would know that Allah, the Exalted, is your Lord and you have no Lord besides Him, so you have no guardians besides Him.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَكَمْ مِنْ قَرْيَةٍ أَهْلَكْناها فَجاءَها بَأْسُنا بَياتاً أَوْ هُمْ قائِلُونَ** "How many a town We have destroyed! Our punishment came to it at night or while they were taking a midday nap." This is a reminder to them of Allah’s way (سنة) that ran among the polytheists of past nations when they took guardians besides Allah; so Allah destroyed them with a punishment He sent down to them by night or by day, and they confessed to their wrongdoing.

*Al-Bayat* (البيات) refers to attacking the enemy at night. *Al-Qa’ilun* (القائلون) is from *al-qaylulah* (القيلولة), which is sleeping in the middle of the day. His saying: **بَياتاً أَوْ هُمْ قائِلُونَ** "at night or while they were taking a midday nap," rather than saying "by night or by day," is seemingly to indicate that the punishment seized them while they were beginning to sleep, feeling secure from the severe divine might (البأس الإلهي) lurking for them, being heedless and caught unawares.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَما كانَ دَعْواهُمْ إِذْ جاءَهُمْ بَأْسُنا إِلَّا أَنْ قالُوا إِنَّا كُنَّا ظالِمِينَ** "Their cry, when Our punishment came to them, was only to say, ‘We were indeed wrongdoers.’" This completes the reminder, explaining that a human being, through his conscience and inner secret, witnesses wrongdoing from himself if he takes guardians besides Allah through polytheism (الشرك). The divine way is to extract this confession from him through the might of punishment if he does not confess it willingly and submit to the station of Lordship; so let him confess by choice, otherwise he will confess by necessity.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَلَنَسْئَلَنَّ الَّذِينَ أُرْسِلَ إِلَيْهِمْ وَلَنَسْئَلَنَّ الْمُرْسَلِينَ** "We will surely question those to whom [Our messengers] were sent, and We will surely question the messengers." The previous explanation indicated that they are tasked with the monotheism (التوحيد) of Allah, the Glorified, and obligated to reject guardians besides Him; they are not left alone in what they did, nor abandoned to do as they please. Since this is the case, they are responsible for what they were commanded of faith and righteous deeds, and what they were tasked with of truthful speech and truthful action. This command and task involve two parties: the Messenger who brought it to them, and the people to whom he came. Therefore, He branched off from the previous discourse on the destruction of towns and the extraction of their confession of wrongdoing by saying: **فَلَنَسْئَلَنَّ الَّذِينَ أُرْسِلَ إِلَيْهِمْ وَلَنَسْئَلَنَّ الْمُرْسَلِينَ**.

It has thus become clear that "those to whom [Our messengers] were sent" refers to the people, and "the messengers" refers to the prophets and messengers عليه‌السلام. The view that "those to whom [Our messengers] were sent" refers to the prophets, and "the messengers" refers to the angels, does not suit the context; for there is no reason to exclude the polytheists from the scope of the questioning when the discourse is about them.

Furthermore, the following verse does not suit that view either. Moreover, the angels did not enter into the previous explanation in any way, neither essentially nor subordinately.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَلَنَقُصَّنَّ عَلَيْهِمْ بِعِلْمٍ وَما كُنَّا غائِبِينَ** "We will surely relate to them with knowledge, and We were not absent." The previous explanation indicated that they are nurtured and governed (مربوبون مدبرون), so they will be questioned about their deeds to be recompensed for what they did. This can only be completed if the Questioner possesses knowledge of their deeds; for the one questioned cannot be trusted not to lie to bring benefit to himself or ward off harm from himself in such a difficult and terrifying station that threatens him with eternal destruction and perpetual loss.

Therefore, He branched off from it His saying: **فَلَنَقُصَّنَّ عَلَيْهِمْ بِعِلْمٍ** "We will surely relate to them with knowledge," etc. "Knowledge" (علم) was made indefinite to emphasize its significance — that it is a knowledge that neither errs nor mistakes. For this reason, He reinforced it by conjoining His saying: **وَما كُنَّا غائِبِينَ** "and We were not absent" to it, to indicate that He was a Witness (شاهداً) and not absent. Even if He appointed angels over them to preserve their deeds by writing, He is encompassing (محيط) of all things.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَالْوَزْنُ يَوْمَئِذٍ الْحَقُّ فَمَنْ ثَقُلَتْ مَوازِينُهُ فَأُولئِكَ هُمُ الْمُفْلِحُونَ** "The weighing on that day is a truth: as for those whose deeds weigh heavy in the scales, it is they who are the felicitous," until the end of the two verses. The two verses inform about the weighing (الوزن), which is the weighing of deeds or the people who act in terms of their deeds. The evidence for this is His saying, the Exalted: **وَنَضَعُ الْمَوازِينَ الْقِسْطَ لِيَوْمِ الْقِيامَةِ** — until He said — **وَكَفى بِنا حاسِبِينَ** "We shall set up the scales of justice on the Day of Resurrection... and We suffice as reckoners" (Al-Anbiya: 47), as it indicates that this weighing is a branch of the reckoning of deeds. Clearer still is His saying: **يَوْمَئِذٍ يَصْدُرُ النَّاسُ أَشْتاتاً لِيُرَوْا أَعْمالَهُمْ فَمَنْ يَعْمَلْ مِثْقالَ ذَرَّةٍ خَيْراً يَرَهُ وَمَنْ يَعْمَلْ مِثْقالَ ذَرَّةٍ شَرًّا يَرَهُ** "On that day mankind will issue forth in various groups to be shown their deeds. So whoever does an atom’s weight of good will see it, and whoever does an atom’s weight of evil will see it" (Az-Zalzalah: 6-8), where He mentioned the deed and attributed the weight to it, whether good or evil.

In short, the weighing is for the deed, not the doer. The verse establishes a weight for the deed, whether it be good or evil. However, His saying, the Exalted: **أُولئِكَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا بِآياتِ رَبِّهِمْ وَلِقائِهِ فَحَبِطَتْ أَعْمالُهُمْ فَلا نُقِيمُ لَهُمْ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ وَزْناً** "They are those who deny the signs of their Lord and the encounter with Him. So their works have failed, and on the Day of Resurrection We will not set up any scale for them" (Al-Kahf: 105), indicates that deeds in cases of nullification (الحبط) — and the discussion on this preceded in the second volume of this book — have no weight at all. What remains for weighing are the deeds of those whose deeds were not nullified.

Thus, the good and evil deeds that have not been nullified have a weight by which they are weighed. However, while the verses consider a heaviness for good and evil deeds, they only consider relative heaviness and base the decisive judgment (القضاء الفصل) upon it. This means that their apparent sense is that good deeds cause the scale to be heavy and evil deeds cause it to be light; it is not that good deeds are weighed and their weight is taken, then evil deeds are weighed and their weight is taken, and then the two weights are compared, such that whichever is greater receives the judgment — so if the weight is for the good deed, the judgment is for Paradise, and if for the evil deed, the judgment is for the Fire. Such a view would necessitate the possibility of the two weights being equal, as in the scales common among us, like the two-pan balance or the steelyard.

Rather, the apparent sense of the verses is that the good deed manifests as heaviness in the scale and the evil deed as lightness in it, as is apparent from His saying: **فَمَنْ ثَقُلَتْ مَوازِينُهُ فَأُولئِكَ هُمُ الْمُفْلِحُونَ وَمَنْ خَفَّتْ مَوازِينُهُ فَأُولئِكَ الَّذِينَ خَسِرُوا أَنْفُسَهُمْ بِما كانُوا بِآياتِنا يَظْلِمُونَ** "As for those whose deeds weigh heavy in the scales, it is they who are the felicitous. As for those whose deeds weigh light in the scales, it is they who have ruined their souls, because they used to wrong Our signs." Similar to it is His saying, the Exalted: **فَمَنْ ثَقُلَتْ مَوازِينُهُ فَأُولئِكَ هُمُ الْمُفْلِحُونَ وَمَنْ خَفَّتْ مَوازِينُهُ فَأُولئِكَ الَّذِينَ خَسِرُوا أَنْفُسَهُمْ فِي جَهَنَّمَ خالِدُونَ** "As for those whose deeds weigh heavy in the scales, it is they who are the felicitous. As for those whose deeds weigh light in the scales, it is they who have ruined their souls, [and] they will remain in hell forever" (Al-Mu’minun: 102-103); and His saying, the Exalted: **فَأَمَّا مَنْ ثَقُلَتْ مَوازِينُهُ فَهُوَ فِي عِيشَةٍ راضِيَةٍ وَأَمَّا مَنْ خَفَّتْ مَوازِينُهُ فَأُمُّهُ هاوِيَةٌ وَما أَدْراكَ ما هِيَهْ نارٌ حامِيَةٌ** "As for him whose deeds weigh heavy in the scales, he shall have a pleasant life. But as for him whose deeds weigh light in the scales, his abode will be the Abyss. And what will show you what it is? A blazing fire" (Al-Qari’ah: 6-11). The verses — as you see — establish heaviness on the side of good deeds always, and lightness on the side of evil deeds always.

From this, the view is supported that there is another standard against which deeds are compared, and the heaviness belongs to it. Whatever deed is good corresponds to it and is weighed by it, and that is the "heaviness of the scale" (ثقل الميزان). Whatever deed is evil does not correspond to it and is not weighed by it, and that is the "lightness of the scale" (خفة الميزان). This is like what we observe in the scales we have; they contain a standard (مقياس), which is the unit of weight like the *mithqal*, placed in one pan, then the commodity is placed in the other pan. If it equals the *mithqal* in weight in some way as indicated by the scale, it is accepted; otherwise, it is inevitably rejected. The *mithqal* is, in reality, the balance (الميزان) by which things are weighed. As for the steelyard, the two-pan balance, and their likes, they are preliminaries for what the *mithqal* clarifies regarding the state of the commodity weighed by it in terms of heaviness and lightness. Similarly, the unit of length, like the cubit or the meter, is a balance by which lengths are weighed; if the length corresponds to the unit standard, it is accepted, otherwise it is rejected.

Thus, in deeds, there is a unit standard (واحد مقياس) by which they are weighed. For prayer (الصلاة), for example, there is a balance by which it is weighed, which is the complete prayer that is the "truth of prayer" (حق الصلاة). For zakat and spending, there is something similar. For speech and saying, there is the "truth of speech" (حق القول) which contains no falsehood. And so on, as alluded to by His saying, the Exalted: **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا اتَّقُوا اللهَ حَقَّ تُقاتِهِ** "O you who have faith! Be wary of Allah with the wariness due to Him" (Al-Imran: 102).

The closest interpretation to this explanation is that the meaning of His saying **وَالْوَزْنُ يَوْمَئِذٍ الْحَقُ** is that the balance (الوزن) by which deeds are weighed on that day is the Truth (الحق). According to the extent to which a deed contains the Truth, it will have consideration and value. Good deeds contain the Truth, so they have heaviness; just as evil deeds are nothing but falsehood, so they have no heaviness. Thus, Allah, the Glorified, weighs deeds on that day by the Truth; whatever Truth the deed contains is its weight and heaviness.

Perhaps this is what is alluded to by "judging with the truth" (القضاء بالحق) in His saying: **وَأَشْرَقَتِ الْأَرْضُ بِنُورِ رَبِّها وَوُضِعَ الْكِتابُ وَجِيءَ بِالنَّبِيِّينَ وَالشُّهَداءِ وَقُضِيَ بَيْنَهُمْ بِالْحَقِّ وَهُمْ لا يُظْلَمُونَ** "The earth will stay bright with the light of its Lord, and the Book will be set up, and the prophets and the witnesses will be brought, and judgment will be made between them with justice, and they will not be wronged" (Az-Zumar: 69). The Book that Allah mentioned will be set up on that day — and it is only set up to judge by it — is the one He alluded to by His saying: **هذا كِتابُنا يَنْطِقُ عَلَيْكُمْ بِالْحَقِّ** "This is Our Book, which speaks against you with the truth" (Al-Jathiyah: 29). Thus, the Book specifies the Truth and what the deed contains of it, and the weighing determines the amount of heaviness.

Accordingly, "weighing" (الوزن) in the verse means "weight" (الثقل) rather than the verbal noun. He expressed it as "scales" (الموازين) — in the plural form — in His saying **فَمَنْ ثَقُلَتْ مَوازِينُهُ** and **وَمَنْ خَفَّتْ مَوازِينُهُ**, which indicates that every individual has many scales due to the diversity of the Truth by which deeds are weighed according to the diversity of the deeds. The Truth in prayer, which is the "truth of prayer," is different from the Truth in zakat, fasting, Hajj, and others; this is obvious. This is what the previous explanation yields.

What the majority of commentators have mentioned regarding the meaning of His saying **وَالْوَزْنُ يَوْمَئِذٍ الْحَقُ** is that "the weighing" (الوزن) is the subject (مبتدأ) in the nominative case, "on that day" (يومئذ) is an adverb, and "the truth" (الحق) is an attribute of the weighing and is its predicate (خبر). The estimation is: "The weighing on that day is the true weighing, which is justice (العدل)." This is supported by His saying, the Exalted, in another place: **وَنَضَعُ الْمَوازِينَ الْقِسْطَ لِيَوْمِ الْقِيامَةِ** "We shall set up the scales of justice on the Day of Resurrection" (Al-Anbiya: 47).

It has also been said that "the weighing" is the subject and its predicate is "on that day," while "the truth" is an attribute of the weighing. The estimation is: "The true weighing is only on the Day of Resurrection." In *al-Kashshaf*, it is said: "Its being in the nominative case — meaning the weighing — is as a subject and its predicate is 'on that day,' and 'the truth' is its attribute; i.e., the weighing on the day Allah questions the nations and their messengers is the true weighing, i.e., justice." (End quote). This is strange, unless it is interpreted by taking his saying "the true weighing..." as a new sentence (استئناف).

**And His saying, the Exalted:** **فَمَنْ ثَقُلَتْ مَوازِينُهُ** "as for those whose deeds weigh heavy in the scales." "Scales" (الموازين) is the plural of "scale" (ميزان) according to the previous explanation, and it is supported by the verse mentioned just now: **وَنَضَعُ الْمَوازِينَ الْقِسْطَ لِيَوْمِ الْقِيامَةِ**. What is more suitable to what the group mentioned regarding the meaning of **وَالْوَزْنُ يَوْمَئِذٍ الْحَقُ** is that it is the plural of "weighed thing" (موزون), which is the deed; although it is possible to make it the plural of "scale" and justify the multiplicity of scales by the multiplicity of deeds weighed by them.

However, there remains the discussion on the commentators' view that "the true weighing is justice" in conceptualizing the meaning of the scales being heavy with good deeds and light with evil deeds. For there is an obscurity in that by which deeds — their good and evil — are weighed. "Equity" (القسط), which is justice (العدل), is an attribute of the weighing and is a description of Allah, the Glorified, as appears from His saying: **وَنَضَعُ الْمَوازِينَ الْقِسْطَ لِيَوْمِ الْقِيامَةِ فَلا تُظْلَمُ نَفْسٌ شَيْئاً وَإِنْ كانَ مِثْقالَ حَبَّةٍ مِنْ خَرْدَلٍ أَتَيْنا بِها وَكَفى بِنا حاسِبِينَ** "We shall set up the scales of justice on the Day of Resurrection, and no soul will be wronged in the least. Even if it be the weight of a mustard seed We shall produce it, and We suffice as reckoners" (Al-Anbiya: 47). The apparent sense of His saying **فَلا تُظْلَمُ** "no soul will be wronged," etc., is that Allah does not wrong them; so equity is His equity and His justice. Thus, equity is not the scale on that day; rather, the setting up of the scales is the setting up of justice on that day. Understand this.

This is what led them to interpret the heaviness of the scales as their preponderance (رجحان) through a type of metaphor (تجوز). Thus, the meaning of the scales being heavy is the preponderance of deeds by being good, and their being light is their lack of preponderance by being evil. The meaning of the verse would be: "The weighing on that day is justice, i.e., the weighing by justice; so those whose deeds preponderate due to the prevalence of good deeds, it is they who are the felicitous. And those whose deeds do not preponderate due to the prevalence of their evil deeds, it is they who have ruined their souls" — i.e., their capital, which is their souls, has gone — "because they used to wrong Our signs" by denying them.

The discussion then returns to the criterion (الملاك) by which a good deed preponderates over an evil deed, especially when deeds are mixed and good and evil deeds are gathered. Good and evil deeds differ in magnitude and insignificance; so what is the criterion by which the prevalence of one category over the other is known? His informing, the Exalted, that the matter of weighing proceeds upon justice indicates that it proceeds in a way that the argument (الحجة) is completed on that day against the servants. Therefore, there must be something that the good deed contains which the evil deed does not, and by it is the preponderance, and by it is known the prevalence of the heavy over the light and the good over the evil when a number of each are gathered with the other; otherwise, it would necessitate saying it is purely arbitrary (جزاف).

All of this supports the possibility we previously presented: that the weighing of deeds is by the Truth, and it is the just weighing. So those whose scales are heavy by their deeds containing the Truth, it is they who are the felicitous. And those whose scales are light due to their deeds not containing the Truth required in servitude (العبودية), it is they who have ruined their souls because they used to wrong Our signs by denying them and not providing themselves with what they would live by on this day; so they destroyed their souls by settling them in the abode of ruin, Hell, which they shall enter — and an evil destination it is.

It has become clear from what we presented, firstly: that the weighing on the Day of Resurrection is the application of deeds to what is the Truth regarding them. According to the extent of their containing it, they entail reward; and if they do not contain it, it is destruction. This weighing is justice, and the discourse in the verses proceeds upon its apparent sense without interpretation (تأويل).

It was said: The meaning of "weighing" is justice, and the heaviness of the scale is the preponderance of the deed; so the discourse is structured as a type of metaphor (استعارة), as mentioned.

It was said: Allah will set up on the Day of Resurrection a scale having a tongue and two pans, and the deeds of the servants — good and evil — will be weighed by it. These people differed on how deeds are weighed, as they are deeds that ceased to exist upon their occurrence, and the restoration of non-existent accidents (الأعراض) is not permissible according to them, besides the fact that they have no weight. So it was said: Only the scrolls of deeds (صحائف الأعمال) are weighed, not the deeds themselves. It was said: Specific effects and signs for the deeds — their good and evil — will appear, and the signs will be weighed in the sight of the people. It was said: Good deeds will appear in beautiful forms and evil deeds in ugly, loathsome forms, and the forms will be weighed. It was said: The soul of the believer and the disbeliever will be weighed, rather than their deeds of good or evil. It was said: The weighing is the manifestation of a person's worth; the heaviness of the scale is his honor and greatness of worth, and the lightness of the scale is his insignificance and humiliation.

These views, despite their divergence, do not rely on an argument from the words of the verses, and they are all not free from basing the described weighing on arbitrariness; for the argument is not completed against the servant by that, as previously alluded to.

Secondly: With respect to every human being, there are scales by which his deeds are weighed. The scale in every category of deed is the Truth contained in that deed — as mentioned — for the Day of Resurrection is the day in which there is no authority except for the Truth and no guardianship (ولاية) except for Allah, the Truth. He, the Exalted, said: **ذلِكَ الْيَوْمُ الْحَقُّ** "That is the True Day" (An-Naba: 39). He, the Exalted, said: **هُنالِكَ الْوَلايَةُ لِلَّهِ الْحَقِّ** "There, guardianship belongs to Allah, the Real" (Al-Kahf: 44). And He said: **هُنالِكَ تَبْلُوا كُلُّ نَفْسٍ ما أَسْلَفَتْ وَرُدُّوا إِلَى اللهِ مَوْلاهُمُ الْحَقِّ وَضلَّ عَنْهُمْ ما كانُوا يَفْتَرُونَ** "There every soul will examine what it has sent ahead, and they will be returned to Allah, their real master, and what they used to fabricate will forsake them" (Yunus: 30).

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ibn al-Daris, al-Nahhas in his *Nasikh*, Ibn Mardawayh, and al-Bayhaqi in *al-Dala’il* narrated through various chains from Ibn Abbas, who said: "Surah al-A’raf was revealed in Mecca."

**I say:** It was also narrated from Ibn Mardawayh from Ibn al-Zubayr.

In it, Ibn al-Mundhir and Abu al-Shaykh narrated from Qatadah, who said: "One verse of al-A’raf is Medinan, which is: **وَسْئَلْهُمْ عَنِ الْقَرْيَةِ الَّتِي كانَتْ حاضِرَةَ الْبَحْرِ** 'Ask them about the town that was situated on the seaside' (until the end of the verse), and the rest of it is Meccan."

**I say:** This is an independent judgment (اجتهاد) from him, and the discussion related to it will follow.

In it, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **فَلَنَسْئَلَنَّ الَّذِينَ أُرْسِلَ إِلَيْهِمْ** (the verse): Ahmad narrated from Mu’awiyah ibn Haydah that the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: **إن ربي داعي وإنه سائلي : هل بلغت عبادي؟ وإني قائل : رب إني قد بلغتهم فليبلغ الشاهد منكم الغائب ـ ثم إنكم تدعون مفدمة أفواهكم بالفدام ـ إن أول ما يبين عن أحدكم لفخذه وكفه.** — "Indeed, my Lord is calling me and He will question me: 'Did you convey to My servants?' And I shall say: 'My Lord, I have indeed conveyed to them.' So let the witness among you convey to the absent. Then you will be called with your mouths gagged with a muzzle; the first thing to speak for one of you will be his thigh and his palm."

In it, al-Bukhari, Muslim, al-Tirmidhi, and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Ibn Umar, who said: The Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: **كلكم راع وكلكم مسئول عن رعيته ـ فالإمام يسأل عن الناس ، والرجل يسأل عن أهله ، والمرأة تسأل عن بيت زوجها ، والعبد يسأل عن مال سيده.** — "Each of you is a shepherd and each of you is responsible for his flock. The leader is questioned about the people, the man is questioned about his family, the woman is questioned about her husband's house, and the servant is questioned about his master's property."

**I say:** There are many narrations with this meaning, and the narrations regarding the questioning on the Day of Resurrection are numerous, reported through the chains of both parties (Sunni and Shi'a); we will mention most of them in a suitable place, if Allah the Exalted wills.

In it, Abu al-Shaykh narrated from Jabir, who said: The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: **يوضع الميزان يوم القيامة فيوزن الحسنات والسيئات ـ فمن رجحت حسناته على سيئاته دخل الجنة ـ ومن رجحت سيئاته على حسناته دخل النار.** — "The scale will be set up on the Day of Resurrection, and good and evil deeds will be weighed. Whoever's good deeds preponderate over his evil deeds will enter Paradise, and whoever's evil deeds preponderate over his good deeds will enter the Fire."

In it, Ibn Abi al-Dunya narrated in *al-Ikhlas* from Ali ibn Abi Talib, who said: **من كان ظاهره أرجح من باطنه خفف ميزانه يوم القيامة ، ومن كان باطنه أرجح من ظاهره ثقل ميزانه يوم القيامة.** — "Whoever's outward is more preponderant than his inward, his scale will be light on the Day of Resurrection; and whoever's inward is more preponderant than his outward, his scale will be heavy on the Day of Resurrection."

**I say:** The two narrations are acceptable in terms of content, but they are not suitable for interpreting the two verses, nor were they narrated for that purpose, because they take "preponderance" (الرجحان) on both sides of good and evil deeds.

In it, Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Aisha, who said: I heard the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله saying: **خلق الله كفتي الميزان مثل السماء والأرض ـ فقالت الملائكة : يا ربنا من تزن بهذا؟ قال : أزن به من شئت ، وخلق الله الصراط كحد السيف ـ فقالت الملائكة : يا ربنا من تجيز على هذا؟ قال : أجيز عليه من شئت.** — "Allah created the two pans of the scale like the heaven and the earth. The angels said: 'O our Lord, whom will You weigh with this?' He said: 'I will weigh with it whom I will.' And Allah created the Bridge (الصراط) like the edge of a sword. The angels said: 'O our Lord, whom will You allow to cross this?' He said: 'I will allow to cross it whom I will.'"

**I say:** Al-Hakim narrated something similar in *al-Sahih* from Salman. The apparent sense of the narration is that the scale on the Day of Resurrection is in the form of the scale existing in this world, made for determining weights. There are scattered narrations that suggest this, and they are intended to bring the meaning closer to simple understandings, as evidenced by the narrations that will follow.

In *al-Ihtijaj*, in the discourse of Hisham ibn al-Hakam from Imam al-Sadiq عليه‌السلام: A heretic (الزنديق) asked him: "Are deeds not weighed?" He said: **لا أن الأعمال ليست بأجسام ـ وإنما هي صفة ما عملوا ، وإنما يحتاج إلى وزن الشيء من جهل عدد الأشياء ، ولا يعرف ثقلها وخفتها ، وإن الله لا يخفى عليه شيء** — "No, for deeds are not bodies; they are only an attribute (صفة) of what they did. One only needs to weigh a thing if he is ignorant of the number of things and does not know their heaviness or lightness, whereas nothing is hidden from Allah." He asked: "Then what is the meaning of the scale (الميزان)?" He replied: **العدل** — "Justice." He asked: "Then what is its meaning in His Book: **فَمَنْ ثَقُلَتْ مَوازِينُهُ**?" He replied: **فمن رجح عمله** — "He whose deed preponderates," (the report).

**I say:** In the narration is support for what we presented in the interpretation of weighing. Among its finest points is his saying عليه‌السلام: "they are only an attribute of what they did," where he عليه‌السلام points out that "deeds" in these contexts do not refer to the natural movements issuing from a human being — as they are common between obedience and disobedience — but rather the attributes occurring to them that are considered in light of social or religious ways and laws. For example, specific movements are called "intercourse" in terms of their own nature, then they are called "marriage" (نكاح) if they agree with social custom or legal permission, and they are called "fornication" (زنا) if they do not agree with that; yet the nature of the movements issued is one. He عليه‌السلام argued for what he mentioned through two ways: first, that deeds are attributes and have no weight; and second, that Allah, the Glorified, does not need to weigh things because He is not characterized by ignorance, exalted is His station.

Some have said: Based on what is true regarding the embodiment of deeds (تجسم الأعمال) in the Hereafter, and the possibility of a good deed's beauty affecting its weight, and that the wisdom in weighing is to terrify the sinner and expose him, and to give glad tidings to the obedient and increase his joy and manifest the ultimate justice — there are aspects of difficulty in the narration, so it must be interpreted if possible, otherwise it should be set aside or interpreted as being said under dissimulation (التقية). (End quote).

**I say:** The discussion on the meaning of the embodiment of deeds has preceded. It is not impossible for deeds to be personified (تتمثل) during the reckoning, and for the divine justice judging them to appear in the form of a scale by which the goods and commodities of deeds are weighed. However, the narration does not negate that; it only negates deeds being worldly bodies (أجسام دنيوية) subject to terrestrial gravity, which manifests in them in the form of heaviness and lightness — first.

Furthermore, the difficulty is based on the manner of weighing being the placement of good deeds in one pan of the scale and evil deeds in the other pan, then weighing and comparing; and you have learned that the verse is far from indicating that at all — second.

In *al-Tawhid*, with its chain from Abu Mu’ammar al-Sa’dani from the Commander of the Faithful عليه‌السلام in a discourse, he said: **وأما قوله : « فَمَنْ ثَقُلَتْ مَوازِينُهُ » و « خَفَّتْ مَوازِينُهُ » فإنما يعني : الحسنات توزن الحسنات والسيئات ـ فالحسنات ثقل الميزان والسيئات خفة الميزان.** — "As for His saying: 'as for those whose deeds weigh heavy in the scales' and 'weigh light in the scales,' it only means: the good deeds. Good and evil deeds are weighed; so good deeds are the heaviness of the scale and evil deeds are the lightness of the scale."

**I say:** Its support for what preceded is clear, for it takes the standard (المقياس) to be the good deed, and it is inevitably a single unit by which others can be measured, and it is nothing but the "truth of the deed" (حق العمل).

In *al-Ma’ani*, with its chain from al-Minqari from Hisham ibn Salim, who said: I asked Abu Abdillah عليه‌السلام about the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: **وَنَضَعُ الْمَوازِينَ الْقِسْطَ لِيَوْمِ الْقِيامَةِ ـ فَلا تُظْلَمُ نَفْسٌ شَيْئاً** "We shall set up the scales of justice on the Day of Resurrection, and no soul will be wronged in the least." He said: **هم الأنبياء والأوصياء.** — "They are the prophets and the executors (الأوصياء)."

**I say:** It was narrated in *al-Kafi* from Ahmad ibn Muhammad from Ibrahim al-Hamdani, attributing it to him عليه‌السلام. The meaning of the hadith is clear from what we presented, for the standard is the truth of deed and belief, and that is what is with them عليه‌السلام.

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain from Sa’id ibn al-Musayyib from Ali ibn al-Husayn عليه‌السلام regarding what he used to exhort with, he said: **ثم رجع القول من الله في الكتاب ـ على أهل المعاصي والذنوب ، فقال عز وجل : « وَلَئِنْ مَسَّتْهُمْ نَفْحَةٌ مِنْ عَذابِ رَبِّكَ ـ لَيَقُولُنَّ يا وَيْلَنا إِنَّا كُنَّا ظالِمِينَ » فإن قلتم أيها الناس إن الله عز وجل ـ إنما عنى بها أهل الشرك فكيف ذلك؟ وهو يقول : « وَنَضَعُ الْمَوازِينَ الْقِسْطَ لِيَوْمِ الْقِيامَةِ ـ فَلا تُظْلَمُ نَفْسٌ شَيْئاً ـ وَإِنْ كانَ مِثْقالَ حَبَّةٍ مِنْ خَرْدَلٍ أَتَيْنا بِها ـ وَكَفى بِنا حاسِبِينَ » فاعلموا عباد الله ـ أن أهل الشرك لا تنصب لهم الموازين ـ ولا تنشر لهم الدواوين ـ وإنما يحشرون إلى جهنم زمرا ، وإنما نصب الموازين ونشر الدواوين لأهل الإسلام.** — "Then the speech from Allah in the Book returned to the people of disobedience and sins, so He, the Mighty and Majestic, said: 'If a breath of your Lord’s punishment were to touch them, they would surely say, "Woe to us! We were indeed wrongdoers."' If you say, O people, that Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, only meant the people of polytheism by it, how can that be? While He says: 'We shall set up the scales of justice on the Day of Resurrection, and no soul will be wronged in the least. Even if it be the weight of a mustard seed We shall produce it, and We suffice as reckoners.' So know, O servants of Allah, that for the people of polytheism, no scales are set up and no registers (الدواوين) are unfolded; they are only gathered to Hell in throngs. The setting up of scales and unfolding of registers is only for the people of Islam," (the report).

**I say:** He عليه‌السلام is referring to His saying, the Exalted: **فَلا نُقِيمُ لَهُمْ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ وَزْناً** "on the Day of Resurrection We will not set up any scale for them" (the verse).

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi, regarding His saying: **وَالْوَزْنُ يَوْمَئِذٍ الْحَقُ** (the verse), he عليه‌السلام said: **المجازاة بالأعمال إن خيرا فخيرا وإن شرا فشرا.** — "The recompense for deeds: if good, then good; and if evil, then evil."

**I say:** This is an interpretation by the result.

In it, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **بِما كانُوا بِآياتِنا يَظْلِمُونَ**, he عليه‌السلام said: **بالأئمة يجحدون.** — "By the Imams they deny."

**I say:** This is of the type of mentioning some instances (المصاديق). There are other narrations with the preceding meanings.
